text: The video demonstrates robotic total pelvic exenteration for locally advanced rectal cancer. Ports are placed for access. The dissection begins by creating a plane underneath the inferior mesenteric vein. The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube, making medial peritoneal cuts. The rectosigmoid and descending colon are reflected off the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected to its root, clipped, and divided beyond the origin of the left colic artery.

The surgeon performs posterior pelvic dissection of the rectal tube, completing lateral peritoneal cuts over the external iliac vessels to facilitate lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The external iliac vessels are identified and dissected, while the ureter is shifted medially. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to the internal iliac vasculature, creating a paravascular space. Nodal tissue between the obturator internus and the internal iliac vasculature is dissected and removed.

The surgeon addresses the medial nodal zone, creating a space between the internal iliac vasculature and pelvic hypogastric structures through gentle dissection. Nodal tissue medial to the internal iliac vasculature is dissected and removed. The superior vesicle pedicle is clipped and divided, followed by the division of blood vessels supplying the prostate and seminal vesicles. The left ureter is clipped and divided near the bladder, then dissected off the retroperitoneum for ureter entry anastomosis. The right lateral pelvic node dissection is performed similarly, with the right ureter clipped, divided, and mobilized. The bladder is mobilized, both umbilical arteries are divided, and the bladder is taken down by entering the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and divides the dorsal venous complex. The prostate is dissected and lifted off the pelvic floor. Bleeding from the dorsal venous complex is controlled, and the area is packed to maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon divides the descending colon using an endostapler and clips the descending colon mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall. The ileocecal junction is mobilized to facilitate the placement of the ileal conduit. Umbilical tape measures the bowel length, and clips mark the proximal and distal ends of the conduit. Endo staplers are used for bowel division.

The surgeon establishes bowel continuity by performing a side-to-side ileo-ileostomy using endo staplers. The cut ends of the ureters are spatulated, and their medial walls are joined with absorbable suture material. Digest ends are inserted into both ureters using a guide wire, and the other ends are placed into the distal end of the ileal conduit.

The surgeon completes the urethra anastomosis using continuous absorbable sutures. The final excision is performed via the perineal route, followed by the creation of an ileal conduit and end colostomy.
